items: **Ancient History:** Time period starts from **earliest times** to 7th Century AD.
**Medieval History:** Time period starts from **8th Century AD to 18th Century** AD, which is the start of Islamic invasion.
**Modern History:** Time period starts from **18th Century AD onwards** till now, which is the start of British Rule.

blocks.text: *James Mill in his book "The History of British India" (1817), divided Indian History into three parts: Hindu, Muslim and British.*
Timeline scale: 1000, 900, 800, 100, 50. Labels: BC (Before Christ)/BCE (Before Common Era) — Birth of Jesus Christ — AD (Anno Domini)/CE (Common Era).
***The number decreases in BC as we move ahead in timeline.*

• Century means: 100 Years
*For example*
• 8th Century BC is 700-800 BC
• 3rd Century AD is: 200-300 AD
• Earth is 4.5 Billion years old

items: It is the current geological era which began 2.58 million years ago and continues to the present.
*It can be divided into two parts:*
**Pleistocene** — Ice Age (Palaeolithic human were living here)
**Holocene** — After Ice Age
**Ancient History** is studied through **Archaeology** (No written evidence and they were obtained through excavation) and **History** (Written evidence were obtained that could be deciphered).
**Archaeology** is divided into **Pre-history** and **Proto-history**.
**Pre-history**: **Stone Age** (Use of stones to make tools) and **Copper Age** (Use of Copper tools).
**Proto-history**: **Bronze Age** (Use of Bronze to make tools and other things.)

blocks.text: *C. J. Thomson developed the Three Age System: Stone, Bronze, and Iron Age.*

items: **Stone Age:** The stone age is categorised on the basis of type of stone tool used.
**Palaeolithic:** Characterized by the use of *large, rough stone tools.*
**Mesolithic:** Marked by the introduction of microliths (micro = *small*; lith = *stone*).
**Neolithic:** Distinguished by the use of *polished stone tools.*

items: Bones of boar, gharial, deer, fish, and fowl were found, which indicates about their domestication.
The most unique feature of Harappan Civilization was development of *Urban Centres*.
Most Harappan sites were divided into two parts

rows:
• Smaller but *higher* town.
• Meant for special public purposes.

• Larger but *lower* town.
• Meant for residential buildings.

items: Both Citadel and Lower Town were *Fortified* (walled) and *physically separated*.

The *citadel* in Harappa was a fortified, elevated area (typically to the west) used for administrative, religious, and ceremonial functions. It served as a secure hub for the ruling class, featuring important buildings like granaries for storage, assembly halls, and structures for public baths, while protecting them from floods and enemies.

blocks.caption: Aerial plan of Mohenjo-Daro labelled: Citadel, Fortification, Lower Town.

blocks.text:
*Exception:*
• *Dholavira (Gujarat) was divided into three parts and Chanhudaro (Pakistan) had no Citadel.*
• *The Citadel within Lothal was not surrounded by a fortification but instead was built on raised mud-brick platform.*

items: *Great Bath (at Mohenjo-daro)* is rectangle shaped tank like structure *(12 m length, 7 m width and 2.4 m depth)*, which might have been used for ritual bathing.
*Gypsum* based *Mortar* was used for waterproofing.
It was the *largest building* in Mohenjodaro.
It was used for *storage of grains*.
Total 12 granaries (6 granaries in each row)
A circular bricks platform was found in harappa.
Most distinctive feature of Harappan Civilization.
*Sumps* or *Cesspits* were made between drains to clean and trap solid waste.
The IVC was known for its advanced *urban planning* and well-structured houses and streets were made in grid pattern.
The houses were made of *burnt bricks* (baked bricks).
Bricks of *standardised ratio (1:2:4)* were used.
*Little pots* of faience (a rare glazed material made from *ground silica*) were used primarily as *luxury containers* for precious substances like perfume or oil.
*Saddle querns* were essential stone tools used for *grinding cereals* like wheat.
Doors were made of wood.
Every house had its own bathroom, many of them had their own *private wells* also.
Grid pattern roads/streets, intersecting at *right angle (90°)*. E.g. Lothal.
*Stone* was predominantly used for constructing the *foundations* of most of *Dholavira* site.
*Limestone slabs* were used as drain covers.
*Canal Traces* were found at *Shortugai*.
Some of the graves contain Potteries and ornaments as belief that it can be used in afterlife.

blocks.text: *Ornaments, consisting of three shell rings, a Jasper Bead and hundred of micro beads, were found near the skull of a male at Harappa.*

items: The Seals often contained *animal motifs* (Short Script) and a *pictographic script*.
The seals usually have a line of writing probably containing the name and title of the owner.
They knew about ships, as *ships* were portrayed in seals.
The Harappan writing system is commonly known as *Indus Script* which was written in *boustrophedon style* (Right to Left then left to right).
The script is not in *alphabetical order* and is *pictographic*.
Most inscriptions are short, the longest inscription contains about *26 signs.*
They were found on jewellery, bone rods, seals, copper tools, jars, and an ancient signboard.
The script is *undeciphered* till date.
*Barter system* was prominent in IVC.
The weights were *uniform.*
Exchange was regulated by a precise system of weights, usually made of a stone called "Chert".
*Sukkur and Rohri hills:* Limestone and Chert blades were mass produced and sent to various Harappan settlements in Sindh.
*Lower denomination of weights:* were in *Binary system*.
*Higher denomination of weights:* were in *Decimal system*.
They worshipped unusual or unfamiliar objects such as *Mother Goddess, Priest King, etc.*
Fire altars at *Kalibangan* and *Lothal* indicate *fire worship*.
*Lingas:* Conical shaped stone objects *worshipped* as symbol of *Shiva*.
Unicorn: One Horned Animal.
Proto Shiva seal (Pashupati).

It is believed that *Aryans* arrived from Central Asia, where one group migrated from Turkey towards Europe and other came towards India.
**Language of Aryans:** *Sanskrit*.
The Aryans are credited with codifying the Vedas.
The word Veda is derived from the *Sanskrit* root word *“Vid”*, which means *“to know”*.
*There are four Vedas: Rigveda, Samveda, Yajurveda and Atharvaveda.*
Oldest Text of Aryans is the *Avesta/Zend-Avesta (Iran)*.
*Apaurusheya:* They are not created by *man but gifted by God*.
They were orally passed *(Shruti)* from one generation to another.

blocks.text: *Only Rig Veda was compiled during Early Vedic Age, rest three Vedas were compiled in later vedic age.*

items: It was the first Veda to be compiled.
It has the *oldest* and *largest* collection of *Sanskrit hymns*.
*Total: 1028* hymns and *10,600* Verses
Hymns are recited by *Hotri*.
*Sukta* were known as hymns in Rigveda.
*Deities:* Indra (known as Purandar), Agni (God of Fire), Vishnu, Soma, and Varuna (God of Rita).
*Female deities:* Aditi (Mother of Gods), Raka (goddess of prosperity), Sinivali (goddess of fertility) and Usha (goddess of dawn).
The terms *nrti* (dance/dancer) and *nrtu* (danseuse/female dancer) are mentioned in the Rig Veda.
The Rigveda mentioned the names of some learned ladies like *Ghosha, Apala* and *Viswavara*, who composed mantras and attained the rank of Rishis.
In the Rig Veda Samhita, use of the terms *Ishukrit* and *Isukara* referred to the maker of Arrows.
*Somras* is an *energy drink*.
It is made up of *10 mandalas* and mandala no. *2-7* were created first.
*Gayatri Mantra* was written by *Sage Vishwamitra* for *Goddess Savitri* (mentioned in *3rd mandala*).
*9th mandala* mentions: *Lord Soma (Lord of Plants)*.
*10th Mandala* mentions about the hymn of *Purusha Shukta*.

**Strong military Organisation:** To solve conflicts over fertile land armies need to be organized.
**Social Stratification (Varna System):** Varna system supported the emergences of Kshatriya who controlled territories.
**Geographical Factors:** Ganga plain had fertile alluvial soil which gave more productivity in agriculture.

items: The king of Chola Dynasty has all the authority.

Chola Empire was divided into: *Mandalams* (province) which was further divided into *Valandu* (districts) → *Nadus* (sub-districts) → *Kurram* (group of villages) → *Ur* (village).
Cholas are known for local (*decentralised*)/village government.
*Uttaramerur inscriptions* describe a well-organized village administration.
Every village was divided into *30 yards (kudumbus)*.
Each yard was governed by a local committee called "*Variyam*"
There were two assemblies:
*Ur:* Assembly of *common people*.
*Sabha:* Assembly of *learned Brahmins* (Agrahars: Land of Brahmins).
The village assembly (Sabha) functioned through specialized committees:
**Eri Variyam** → Tank & irrigation management.
**Tottavariyam** → Gardens.
**Pon Variyam** → Gold & finance.
**Nyaya Variyam** → Justice.
Members of committees were selected through the *Kudavolai system* (lottery method), famously described in the Uttaramerur inscriptions (Chingleput,Tamil Nadu).
People with property or land were privileged to take part in elections.
Every member of the committee was appointed for *3 years*.

It features 24 intricately carved stone wheels (12 pairs). Islamic rulers used to hire Slaves from Samarkand region (Present day Uzbekistan) and convert them to Islam then eventually set them to higher position to become rulers.
The Abbasid Empire disintegrated around the 10th century and Independent Kingdoms emerged.
**Manshur:** A formal letter issued by Khalifa to Sultans (ruler) to rule over a certain region.

blocks.text: Dynasties that Ruled Transoxania and Khorasan Region

items: Islamic rule brought the *Umayyads and Abbasids*. It was followed by independent Persian/Turkic dynasties.
**Samanids** (819-999 AD)
**Ghaznavids** (977 AD-1186 AD)
**Seljukids**
**Khwarazmian**
**Mongols** (founded by *Genghis Khan*)
**Timurids** (founded by *Timur*)
**Alap-Tigin** (a Turk slave) initially captured Ghaznavid region, but it was Sabuktigin, his son-in-law who later set an independent dynasty (the real founder).
Sabuktigin and his son, Mahmud of Ghaznavi / Ghazni captured *Zabulistan and Kabulistan region* of Afghanistan from Hindu Shahi Rulers in the late 10 Century AD.
*Jayapala* of Hindu Shahi Dynasty (Peshawar Region) was defeated by Sabuktigin.
Mahmud of Ghaznavi attacked *Waihind/Udabhandapura* (a strong base of Hindu Shahi rulers after Peshawar) in *999 AD*.
This invasion took place in Sindh part of India.
He came from Arab and killed *Raja Dahir* (last Hindu ruler of Sindh).
He was the first to introduce *jizya* in Indian subcontinent.

blocks.text: __Mahmud of Ghazni → 2nd Islamic/1st Turk Invasion (998 AD-1030 AD)__

items: Turks came from *Turkmenistan*.
He attacked *17 times* (1001 AD-1027 AD).
**Battle of Peshawar (1001 AD):** It took place between *Mahmud of Ghazni* (it was his first attack) and the Hindu Shahi ruler *Jayapala (defeated)*, near Peshawar.
Jayapala committed suicide called 'Funeral Pyre' in which King and his followers accept Death.

**Battle of Waihind or Chaach (1008 AD):** A series of conflicts between Ghazni and Anandapal, Son of Jayapala. In this Battle, Ghaznavi captured the fort of Bhimnagar and Nagarkot (Present in Pakistan) which marked the entry of Ghaznavid Empire into Lahore, Taxila and Punjab region.
**Attack on Somnatha Temple (1025 AD):** Ghazni invaded *Somnath* in his *16th attack* and it was his last major expedition outside Punjab.
Iqta System
Excellent Breed of Horses
*"Spirit of Ghazi"* which means that non-muslims are Kafirs and Islam has to eliminate them to capture their lands.

blocks.text: Expeditions of Mahmud of Ghazni in India

items: **Attacked Mathura in 1015 AD:** *Defeated Kokalla II* of Kalachuri Dynasty who gave strong resistance to Ghazni.
Ghazni plundered Mathura.
**Attacked Kannauj:** *Rajyapala,* Gurjar-Pratihara Ruler of Kannauj gave no resistance to Ghazni and fled from Kannauj.
Rajyapala was replaced by *Trilochanpala*.
Ghazni again attacked in 1019 and defeated Trilochanpala of Kannauj.
When Ghazni again attacked in *1021 AD*, he faced a strong resistance from Chandela Ruler Vidhyadhar with a strong army.
Following the Somnath Raid, the *Jats/ Khokhars* attacked his returning army, prompting Mahmud to launch a final punitive naval campaign against them in 1027 AD, near the Indus River.
**Death: 1030 AD**.

blocks.text: Writers during the reign of Mahmud of Ghazni

items: *Ferishta* was a historian of the 17th Century CE wrote about history of Islamic rule in India.
*Al-Utbi* was a Persian historian and court chronicler during the reign of Mahmud of Ghazni. He is best known for his work *Kitab al-Yamini*.

items: *Chauhans* of Shakambhari were ruling over **Rajasthan Region**.
*Solankis* were ruling over **Gujarat region**.
*Parmars* were ruling over **Malwa Region**.
*Tomars* were ruling over **Delhi**.
*Chandelas* were ruling in **Mahoba** and **Khajuraho Region**.
*Anangpal Tomar* founded the *Tomara Dynasty*. Delhi first became a capital under their rule only.
Vigraharaja (a Chauhan Ruler) captured Chittorgarh and then Delhi from Tomars (1151 AD).
He allowed Tomars to rule over Delhi as feudatories.
*Prithviraj III or Prithviraj Chauhan* ascended the throne at the age of 11 in 1171 AD.
Prithviraj invaded *Jejakabhukti (Bundelkhand)*, defeating the Chandela king Paramardi and sacking the capital, Mahoba.
He launched a campaign against the Chalukyas (Solankis) of Gujarat, ruled by Bhima II which later ended in a Peace Treaty.
Prithviraj had strained relations with Jaichand of Kannauj.
*Prithviraj Raso (a Brajbhasha epic poem)*, written by Chand Bardai mentions the abduction of *Samyukta*, daughter of Jaichand by Prithviraj.

blocks.text: __Muhammad Ghori → 3rd Islamic/2nd Turk Invasion (1175 AD-1206 AD)__

items: *Persian name: Mu'izz al-Din Muhammad*.
He was the Younger brother of Ghiyasuddin Muhammad (Ghurid Empire).
He conquered Peshawar from the Ghaznavid Empire (the ruler of Ghaznavid Empire was Khusrau Malik).
*His first attack took place in: 1175 AD* (in Multan) and *1178 AD (Battle of Anhilwara)* in Gujarat, where he was defeated by *Mularaja II, Bhima II* and their mother *Naiki Devi*.
Captured *Tabarhinda fort* of *Bhatinda* in 1191 but later got defeated by Prithviraj Chauhan in *1st Battle of Tarain (1191 AD)* & later defeated Prithviraj in *2nd Battle of Tarain (1192 AD)*.
*Qutb-ud-din Aibak* (commander of Ghori) helped him in 2nd Battle of Tarain.

blocks.text: *Jai Chand is said to have invited Md. Ghori to attack Prithviraj Chauhan.*

items: This battle was fought between *Ghori* and *Raja Jaichand*, where Ghori won the battle.
Following this battle, Ghori conquered Kannauj in 1198 AD and captured *Fort of Asni*.
*Slaves of Ghori: Qutb-ud-din-Aibak, Yalduz, Kubacha.*
Muhammad Ghori was killed by the Khokhar Tribe in 1206 AD.
*Yalduz* **(Slave of Ghori)** took over Ghazni.
*Multan* and *Uchch region* was administered by Kubacha **(Slave of Ghori)**
Lahore was under the rule of Qutub-ud-din Aibak.
He was *not a slave of Ghori* but came to India with him.
Initially he took governorship under Rajput rulers and gradually, he consolidated his power in the region.
He *destroyed Nalanda* and *Vikramashila University*.
*Conquered Odantpuri Fort* (a Buddhist vihara) of Bihar in 1193 CE and occupied Lakhnauti region in Bengal in 1204 CE.
During Assam Expedition to occupy Tibet, Bakhtiyar Khilji was assassinated by his own Military General *Ali Mardan Khan* at *Devkot* in 1206 CE.
After Ali Mardan, Ghiyasuddin Iwaz succeeded the throne who accepted the suzerainty of Iltutmish.
*Malik Jani* was appointed as Governor of Bihar by Iltutmish.
Finally in 1227 CE *Nasiruddin Mahmud* (Son of Iltutmish) invaded Bengal and Bihar by defeating Ghiyasuddin Iwaz.

He ruled from Lahore (it was the capital city).
He was given the title of *'Lakh Baksh'* (the giver of Lakhs).
*Death: 1210 AD*, while playing *Chaugan* (now known as *Polo*).
*Architecture constructed by him:*
*Mosques: Quwwat-ul-Islam* at Delhi and *Adhai din ka Jhonpra* at Ajmer.
He also began construction of *Qutub Minar* (he was only able to complete the construction of basement), in honour of *Sufi Saint Khwaja Qutbuddin Bakhtiar Kaki*.
*He patronized writers like:* Hasan-un-Nizami (author of Tajul-Ma'asir) and *Fakhruddin*.
It is an *Indo-Islamic* structure having 5-storeys (*73* or *72.5* m high)
UNESCO world heritage site: 1993.
*Qutub-ud-din Aibak* (laid foundation).
Iltutmish (added three more stories to it).
Firoz Shah Tughlaq (repaired and built the top story).
*Quwwat-ul-Islam*: It is made in *corbel arch* technique.
*Adhai din ka Jhonpra*: Earlier it had Jain Monasteries. *(Basadis)*
He was son-in-law of *Qutb-ud-din* and was governor of Budaun (UP).
He defeated Aram Shah (son of *Qutb-ud-din Aibak*) in 1211 AD to ascend the throne.
He shifted his capital from *Lahore* to *Delhi*.
He saved Delhi Sultanate from the wrath of *Genghis Khan* (Genghis also *attacked Transoxiana* region in 1219 AD. He *died in 1227 AD*).
*Nizam-ul-Mulk* was his *Wazir* (Prime Minister).
He patronized scholars like *Minhaj-i-Siraj* (wrote: *Tabaqat-i-Nasiri*).
He set up the official nobility of 40 loyal slaves known as *Chahalgani*. These were *Turkish* slaves.
He built *Hauz Shamsi*, a Water Reservoir in Delhi.
He introduced *Silver coin (Tanka)* and *Copper coin (Jital)*.

He introduced the *Iqta system*. Under this, a part of *land (iqta)* was given to military commander (*Iqtadar/Muqti*) in exchange for military service, revenue collection, and local administration, essentially functioning as temporary, transferable revenue assignments rather than hereditary land grants.
She was the daughter of *Iltutmish*.
*She was the first and the only woman ruler of the Delhi Sultanate.*
Altunia, Governer of Bhatinda refused to accept suzerainty of Razia. Razia and *Yakut* (an Ethiopian slave) marched against Altunia.
Altunia murdered Yakut and imprisoned Razia in *Quila Mubarak of Bhatinda* (Later Altunia and Razia got married).
In 1240 AD, Razia and Altunia became the victims of a conspiracy by *Khokhar tribe* and were assassinated near Kaithal (Haryana).
*Minhaj-i-Siraj*, in his book, *Tabaqat-i-Nasiri*, writes - "The king was entirely slaved by dissipation and debauchery that Razia was more able and qualified than her brothers".
*Original name: Baha-ud-Din*.
He was earlier a *Naib* under *Nasiruddin Mahmud*.
He broke the power of *Chalisa* (abolished *Chahalgani*) system and restored the prestige of the crown.
He established the military department *Diwan-i-Arz*.
*Title taken: Zil-i-Ilahi* (Shadow of Allah) and *Ulugh Khan* → Balban considered himself as a descendant of *Afrosiyab*.
He introduced *Sijda* (prostration before the monarch) and *Paibos* (kissing the feet of monarch) as the normal forms of salutation.
He started *"Blood and Iron Policy"*.
He started *Nowruz* (Iranian or Parsi New Year) in Delhi Sultanate.
Indian Muslims and Hindus never attained high positions during his reign.

*Tughril Beg*, the Governor of Bengal (*Lakhnauti*) under Sultan Balban, revolted against him and declared himself as an independent ruler of Bengal in 1279. Later, he was assassinated by Balban with the help of his son Bughra Khan.
He was the last ruler of Slave Dynasty and grandson of Balban.

rows: Incharge of all other departments except Finance/deputy to Sultan

items: Khiljis were *Turks* but did not belong to the Ilbari tribe or Shamshi tribe.
He was the *founder* of Khilji Dynasty.
He was a very lenient ruler and allowed Hindus and non-turks to attain high positions.
*Real name: Ali Gurshasp*
He was the *nephew* and *Son-in-law* of Jalal-ud-din Khilji.
Alauddin khilji killed his uncle and Sultan, Jalaluddin Khilji, after his successful 1296 raid on Devagiri.
Alauddin lured Jalaluddin to Kara, claiming to present the treasures, and had him murdered during a meeting on a boat on the Ganges river.
He reversed the policies of Jalal-ud-din.
Nushrat Khan was the brother of Alauddin.
He held the title of *Amir-i-Tuzuk* (Master of Ceremonies) during the reign of Jalaluddin Khilji.
He *abolished* iqta system in the Delhi Sultanate.
*Gujarat (1298 AD):* Appointed Ulugh and Nushrat Khan & captured Ahilwara.
Defeated *Rai Karan* (Vaghela King of Gujarat) and put his wife to Harem.
**Ranthambore (1301 AD)** → Then King: Hammir Dev Chauhan (Evidence of first *Jauhar* found during this time).
*Mewar (1303 AD)* → *Capital: Chittor (King: Ratan Singh).* Chittorgarh Fort, built by King Chitranga Mori (also known as Chitrangda) of the Maurya dynasty and later restructured by Rana Kumbha.
*Malwa (1305 AD)*
*Jalor (1311 AD)*

blocks.text: *Alauddin bought him from Gujarat Market at 1000 dinars hence Malik Kafur is also known as 'Hazar Dinari'.*

items: Alauddin's army was led by *Malik Kafur* (he was a eunuch).
He defeated:
*Ramachandra* (Yadava Dynasty ruler of Devagiri) (1308 AD), Khilat and Chhatra was given to Ramchandra by Alauddin
*Prataparudra Deva* (Kakatiya Dynasty ruler of Warangal) (1310 AD)
*Vira Ballala III / Ballala Deva* (Hoysala Dynasty ruler of Dwarasamudra) (1311 AD)
*Vira Pandya* (Pandya Dynasty ruler of Madurai) (1311 AD), Malik Kafur became Malik Naib (personal representative of Sultan) after this victory.
Title taken by Alauddin after the Deccan expedition: *Sikandar-i-Sani (Second Alexander)*.
After the Deccan expedition, Malik Kafur built a mosque *(Alia al-Din Khaldji) in Rameshwaram*.
Alauddin conferred the title of *Tuti-e-Hind* (Parrot of India) to Khusrau, his favourite court poet.
He started *Qawwali* in India. He also invented Tabla and Sitar.
Khusrau gave *Sultan-e-Jahan* title to Khilji.
*His famous books: Tughlaq Nama* and *Nuh-i-Sipihr*.
He was the first to mention Jauhar in Persian (*in his book Khazain-ul-Futuh*).
*Dagh* (the branding of horse) and *Chehra* (also known as *Huliya*, for identification of his soldiers).
Alauddin Started the system of *Cash Payment (Naqdi)* to Soldiers.
Weakened the power of Intermediaries like Rana and Rawats of Rajput rulers.
Weakened the power of the Village Head called *Muqaddam* or *Chaudhary*.
Imposed heavy taxes: Ordered all lands to be measured by a Special Officer: *Mustakharaj* (one who collected the revenue). The share of state was then fixed based on this measurement.
*Taxes Imposed: Jaziya* (levied on Non-Muslims) and *Zakat* (levied on Muslims).

*He introduced three new taxes: Ghari* (house tax), *Charai* (a tax levied on use of grasslands for grazing animals), and *Kharaj* (a land tax imposed on peasants, amounting to *50% of their produce*).
*Three markets setup by Alauddin:*
Food Grains Market: *Mandi*
Cloths/Costly Items Market: *Sarai-i-Adl.*
Horses, Slaves and Cattle Market
*Each market was under control: Shahna* (high officer) who maintained a register of the merchants, shopkeeper and the prices.
He fixed the price of all products in order to maintain a huge standing army.
*Dirham* or *Dam* were used as currency.
All products were brought to the Market by *Banjaras*. *Shahna* were appointed to look after Banjaras.
Network of spies (intelligence officers) started by Alauddin were: *Munhiyans* and *Barid*.
Alauddin started *"Alai gaz"* - a measuring unit.

items: Started land measurement in units of Bigha and Biswa (*1 Bigha = 20 Biswa*).
Started land confiscation *(like waqf)* and converted them to agricultural land.
Villages were brought under Khalisa (land not covered under Iqta), also Doab region was brought under Khalisa.
**Land Revenue:** Half of the agricultural produce (called *Kharaj*); directly collected revenue from farmers, and eliminated intermediaries like khots and muqaddams.
*Alai Fort*
*Hauz Khas (tank)*
*Alai Darwaja (entrance of Qutub Minar)*
*Palace of thousand Pillars (Hazar Sutun)*
The city *Siri* which was *second* of the seven cities of Delhi (1st city of Delhi: *Qila Rai Pithora* established by *Tomar Dynasty*).
*Tomb of Alauddin: Delhi.*
*He was patron of:* Art and learning.
*In 1316:* Malik Kafur *(Hajar-Dinari)* seized the throne after Alauddin's death.
*Alauddin had two sons: Mubarak* (1316-20 AD) and *Omar*. Mubarak firstly killed Malik Kafur and then Omar.
Mubarak was finally killed by his own slave Khusrau Khan in 1320 AD.
Last King of Khilji Dynasty *Khusrau Khan* was killed by *Ghazi Malik* (took title: Ghiyasuddin Tuglaq).
He built the city of *Tughlaqabad* (3rd city of Delhi) and the *Tughlaqabad Fort*.
He died in an accident and was succeeded by his son Jauna (Ulugh Khan) who assumed the title *Muhammad bin Tughlaq*.
*Original name: Jauna*.
He had the largest kingdom of all.
He increased taxation in Doab region in 1325 to fund his projects, including his large army.
He is also known as *"wisest fool"*, due to his failed policies.
He transferred the capital from *Delhi* to *Daulatabad* (also known as *Devagiri*) in 1327 and later he again reversed his decision.
He also gave *Sondhar* loan (cash in exchange of gold) and *Taccavi* loan as agricultural loans.
**Religious Visit:** He was the first Sultan to visit the *tomb of Moinuddin Chishti (Ajmer)*.
He introduced Token currency (copper and brass) in *1329* with high value.
*Khorasan Expedition in 1329.*
*Qarachil Expedition in 1330.*
Muhammad bin Tughlaq appointed people from variety of background to high administrative positions {mentioned by *Ziauddin Barani* in his book *Tarikh-i-Firuz Shahi* (Fatwa-i-Jahandari)}.
He also setup an agricultural department: *Diwan-i-Kohi*.
*Ibn Battuta:* A traveller from Morocco who wrote *Rihla (in arabic language)* (In his book, Muhammad Bin Tughlaq is mentioned as one of the four great/powerful rulers of the world).
**Postal System:** (Described by Ibn Battuta):
*Uluq:* Horse post (Stations were kept at a distance of 4 miles).
*Dawa:* Foot post (There were 3 stations per mile). The foot runners carried a *Copper Bell* to announce their arrival.
Built the city of *Jahanpana (4th city of Delhi)* and the *Begumpuri Mosque*.
Built the Mausoleum (Dargah) of Nizamuddin Auliya in Delhi.
He was the cousin of Muhammad Bin Tughlaq.
His coronation was done at *Thatta (Sindh, Pakistan)* in 1351 AD.
He wrote his *autobiography titled Fatuhat-i-Firozsahi*.
Soldiers were not paid in cash but by assignments on land revenue of villages called *Vajeha* or *Wajh*.
The Iqta system was made hereditary.
*Kharaj:* Land tax (1/10 of the produce).
*Zakat:* 2.5% tax on property.
*Jizya:* Levied on Non-Muslims (It became a separate tax during his time).
*Khams:* 1/5 booty captured during war.
*Irrigation Tax (Haque-i-Sharb):* He repaired canals and imposed *Hasil-i-Sharb* (water tax).
Firoz Shah introduced two more types of coins : *Adha:* Represented **50% (half)** of a jital. *Bikh:* Represented **25% (one-quarter)** of a jital.
*Ulema:* Religious officer.
He built new cities: *Fatehabad, Hisar, Jaunpur* {in the name of Muhammad Bin Tughlaq (Jauna)}and Firozabad (5th city of Delhi).
Built the 5th storey of Qutub Minar
He was the first Sultan to start the translation of Hindu *Religious texts into Persian*.
He established a hospital at Delhi: *Dar-ul-Shifa*.
New departments were set up by him: *Diwan-i-khairat* (Department of Donations) and *Diwan-i-Bandagan* (Department of slaves).
FST's Wazir (PM) *Khan-i-Jahan Maqbul* took over the administration after his death.
After him, his son Khan-i-Jahan II became Wazir but faced conflict with Prince Muhammad.
Nasiruddin Mahmud Shah became the Sultan.
He invaded during the reign of the last ruler of Tughlaq Dynasty *Mahmud Shah Tuglaq*/Nasiruddin Mahmud Shah.
After defeating the Delhi army, Timur appointed Khizr Khan as the ruler of Multan.
**Death:** Timur died in 1405 AD while on his way to conquer China.
*Founder: Khizr Khan* (1414 AD - 1421 AD) → Title taken: *Rayat-e-Ala*.
They were known as *"Kulah-Daran"* (cap wearers).
Khizr Khan defeated Sultan *Daulat Khan* and occupied Delhi and founded Sayyid dynasty.
*Tarikh-i-Mubarak Shahi* was written by *Yahya-bin-Ahmad Sirhindi*, where he tells that Sayyids were the descendants of *Prophet Muhammed*.
*Alam Shah* was the last ruler of the Sayyid dynasty.
Lodi's were of *Afghan origin*.
*Founder: Bahlol Lodi* (1451 AD-1489 AD).
He is also known as *Nizam Shah*.
He founded Agra in *1504* and later made it his capital in *1506*.
*He introduced: Gaz-i-Sikandari* (Sikandar's yard) of *32 digits* to measure cultivated fields.
Translated *Ayurvedic texts to Persian*.
He was a poet and composed poems in Persian, under the name *Gulrukhi*.
*He gave orders to build: Moth ki Masjid (in Delhi).*
He was the last ruler of Delhi Sultanate.
The *Battle of Khatoli* was fought in 1517–1518 between the Rajput forces of *Mewar* under Rana Sanga and the *Sultanate of Delhi* under Ibrahim Lodi where Rana Sanga had a decisive victory.
Babur was invited to attack on Ibrahim Lodi by *Daulat Khan Lodi* (who was the *governor of Punjab* under Ibrahim Lodi) and *Alam khan* (Ibrahim Lodi's uncle).
He fought and lost the *First Battle of Panipat* against *Babur* in *1526 AD*.

blocks.text: Some Key Departments under Delhi Sultanate

items: The meaning of Vijayanagara: *City of Victory*.
*Capital*: *Hampi* (which is derived from the name of local mother goddess *Pampadevi*) is situated on the banks of the Tungabhadra River.
The ruins of Hampi were brought to light in 1800 by *Colin Mackenzie* (the first Surveyor General of India).
Hampi was declared as *UNESCO World Heritage Site* in the year *1986*.

blocks.caption: Map of India showing Bahmani kingdom (yellow) and Vijayanagar kingdom (green) with ancient towns; includes Ahmadnagar, Bidar, Gulbarga, Bijapur, Warangal, Devagadi (Daulatabad), Golconda, Hampi, Bellary, Vijayanagar, Halebid, Mysore, Kanchi, Kozhikode, Tanjore, Madurai, Kollam.

items: *Alexander Greenlaw* took the first detailed photographs of archaeological remains of Vijayanagara (Hampi).
*John Marshall* began the conservation of the archaeological remains of Vijayanagara (Hampi).
Contemporaries describe this empire as *Karnataka Samrajyamu*.
Early Vijayanagara rulers called themselves as *Hindu Suratrana*.
*Vijayanagara* kings were also know as *"Raya"*.
*Vijayanagara* → *Narapati* (Lord of men).
*Odisha* rulers → *Gajapati* (Lord of elephants).
*Bahmani* rulers → *Ashvapati* (Lord of horses).
Local communities of merchants → *Kudirai Chettis*.
Muhammad Bin Tughlaq was the ruler of Delhi Sultanate when this empire was established.

blocks.text: Four Important Dynasties of Vijayanagara Empire
Harihara I and Bukka *(1336 AD-1356 AD)*

items: They are the founders of Sangama Dynasty (1336 AD-1485 AD) *(sons of Sangama)*.
They were the feudatories of *Kakatiyas* and later became ministers in the court of *Kampili*.
Title taken by *Harihara I: Karnataka Vidhya Vilas*.
Bukka is credited with renaming *Vidyanagar* to *Vijayanagara*.
*Deva Raya I* and *Deva Raya II* succeeded Harihara and Bukka.
Conflict with Bahmani Kingdom: He was initially defeated by Firuz Shah Bahmani.
As part of the peace treaty, Deva Raya I gave his daughter in marriage to Firuz Shah. He also lost the territory of *Bankapur* as *dowry*.
In a subsequent battle, Deva Raya I successfully defeated Firuz Shah Bahmani.
The *Hiriya Canal* was developed during his reign. It drew water from the Tungabhadra river and irrigated the valley, supplying water to the "Sacred Centre" and the "Urban Core"
In 1443, he crossed the Tungabhadra river to try and recover *Mudgal* and *Bankapur* (regions south of the Krishna river).
He built Kamalapuram Tank . It was built to support the city's water needs.
Fernao Nuniz, a Portuguese traveller in his work noted that the kings of Ceylon (Sri Lanka), Pulicat, Pegu, and Tenasserim paid tribute to Deva Raya II.
He is the founder of Saluva Dynasty (1485 AD-1505 AD).

blocks.text: Veera Narasimha/ Narasa Nayaka *(1505 AD-1509 AD)*

items: He was the founder of Tuluva Dynasty (1505 AD-1570 AD).
He was placed on the throne by Saluva Timma, the Chief Minister of Veera Narasimha.
Considered the greatest king of the Vijayanagara Empire.
Title taken: *Yavanaraja Sthapnacharya* (Restorer of the Yavana/Bidar Kingdom), *Abhinava Bhoja, Andhra Pitamaha / Andhra Pratimah* (Grandfather of Andhra) .
He built: *Vijaya Mahal* (House of Victory), *Hazara Rama Temple*, and *Vitthal Swami Temple* (dedicated to Lord Vishnu).
Founded a suburban township named *Nagalapuram* (after his mother).
He was a gifted scholar in Telugu and Sanskrit.
His works include: *Amuktamalayada* (Telugu work on statecraft) and *Jambavati Kalyanam* (Sanskrit drama).
His court was adorned by the *Ashtadiggajas* (8 great poets):

He gave permission to Albuquerque (a Portuguese Governor) to build a fort at *Bhatkal (port)*.
He defeated the rulers of *Odisha (Gajapatis)* in his early days.
He defeated Ismail Adil Shah (Bijapur) and restored the Raichur Doab (region between Krishna and Tungabhadra) in 1520 AD.
After the death of Krishna Deva Raya, his brother Achyuta Deva Raya ascended the throne.
Upon Achyuta's death in 1542, his nephew Sadashiva Raya was appointed Emperor.
However, the real power lay in the hands of Aliya Rama Raya (Krishna Deva Raya's son-in-law), who acted as the caretaker/regent.
Due to much interference by Aliya Rama Raya in the internal affairs of the Deccan Sultanates, they decided to attack Vijayanagara.
Also known as the *Battle of Rakshasi Tangadi* or the *Battle of Bannihatti*.
In this battle, the combined army of *Bijapur, Ahmednagar,* and *Golconda* attacked and ransacked the Vijayanagara Empire.
After the battle, the Vijayanagara empire continued to rule in a very small part of its former territory.
The capital was shifted to *Penukonda* and later to *Chandragiri* to survive the attacks.
The last ruler was *Sri Ranga III (Aravidu Dynasty)*.
*Ayagar System:* Each village was organised as an independent unit for the operation of rural administration, and *12 officers* were appointed for their operations.
*Administrative Hierarchy:* *Rajya* (Province) → *Mandalam* (District) → *Sthala* (Group of Villages) → *Grama* (Village).
*Amara-Nayaka System:* Amara-Nayakas were military commanders who were given territories to govern.
This system flourished under Krishna Deva Raya.
The term *Amaram* refers to the land given to them.

items: Babur defeated *Ibrahim Lodhi* in the *1st Battle of Panipat* on April 21, 1526 AD and was the founder of *Mughal Dynasty*.
*Original name:* *Zahiruddin Muhammad Babur*.
*Title assumed:* *Padshah* (in 1507 AD at Kabul).
*Lineage:* He had the blood of two great conquerors:
**Father's side:** Descendant of *Timur*.
**Mother's side:** Descendant of *Genghis Khan (Mongol)*.
Babur was from *Uzbekistan*.
When he was *12 years old*, he became the ruler of *Ferghana* in *1494*.
He was forced to leave his throne after the Uzbegs, another Mongol group, invaded.
He conquered Kabul in *1504 AD*.
**Entry into India:** Daulat Khan Lodi invited Babur to India to fight against Ibrahim Lodi.
Before Panipat, Babur conquered Bajaur and Bhera in 1519. This was the first time he used Gunpowder in this region.
In battles, he was the *first to use gun powder massively*.
*1st Battle of Panipat (21 April, 1526 AD):* Defeated Ibrahim Lodi. This victory laid the foundation of the Mughal Dynasty).
*Battle of Khanwa (1527 AD):* He defeated Rana Sanga of Mewar at Khanwa (Babur assumed the title “Ghazi” after this victory).
*Battle of Chanderi (1528 AD):* He defeated Medini Rai of Chanderi.
*Battle of Ghagra (1529 AD):* He defeated Afghans (Mahmud Lodi).
*1530:* He died at Agra. Initially buried in Agra (Aram Bagh), but his remains were later moved to Kabul (Afghanistan).
__Military Strategy-__
Babur was the first to use Gunpowder massively in Indian warfare.
Tulughma Formation: His specific war strategy used in Panipat. It involved dividing the army into:

blocks.caption: Tulughma Formation

items: Harawal (Center/Vanguard)
Jaranghar (Left Wing) & Baranghar (Right Wing)
Tulughma (Flanking units to encircle the enemy).
*Tuzuk-i-Baburi* in *Turkish*.
It was translated to *Persian* language, named → *Baburnama* by *Abdur Rahim Khanekhana*.
It was translated to English by *Madam Beveridge*.

Humayun (1530 AD-1540 AD and 1555 AD-1556 AD)

items: He was the *eldest son* of Babur.
He had a broken reign (ruled in two parts).
He ascended the throne in *1530 AD*.
His succession was challenged by his brothers: *Kamran, Hindal, Askari* and *Afghans* as well.
Humayun’s brother Kamran controlled Afghanistan and Punjab regions.
He fought two battles against *Sher Shah Suri* (Afghani ruler) at *Chausa* in *1539 AD* and at *Kannauj/Bilgram* in *1540 AD*, where he was completely *defeated* by Sher Shah.
Humayun, after losing the battle took refuge at *Safavid Dynasty* (Iran).
Rani Karnawati of Mewar once sent a Rakhi to Humayun seeking protection against Bahadur Shah of Gujarat.
His sister *Gulbadan Begum*, wrote his biography “*Humayunama*”.
After Sher Shah’s death, Humayun regained his empire in 1555 AD.
He built *Din Panah* at *Delhi* as his 2nd capital.
He died in *1556 AD* after falling from the stairs of his library.
*Original name:* *Farid Khan*.
He was the son of *Hasan Khan*, the Jagirdar of Sasaram (Bihar).
Ibrahim Lodhi transferred his father’s Jagir to him.
Battles of Sher Shah Suri:
*1539 AD:* He defeated Humayun in *Battle of Chausa* and then assumed the title “*Sher Shah*”.
*1540 AD:* He defeated Humayun in *Battle of Kannauj/Bilgram* and annexed Kannauj under him.

items: **Built by:** *Bega Begum* {also known as *Haji Begum* (his widow)}.
**Architect:** *Mirak Mirza Ghiyath*.
He introduced the *Rupia (silver coin)*.
Fixed standard weights and measures across the empire.
**Direct Control:** Removed Zamindars in many areas to collect taxes directly.
*Pargana* (an administrative unit comprising several villages) during Sher Shah was led by *Shiqdar*.
Land was measured accurately.
Tax was fixed at *1/3rd* of the average produce.
Introduced *Patta* (Title deed) and *Qabuliyat* (Agreement deed) to define peasants rights and taxes.
Built the Grand Trunk Road connecting Chittagong (Bangladesh) to Peshawar.
*Death:* In 1545 AD during the conquest of Kalinjar ( due to a gunpowder explosion).
He built *Purana Quila* (Old Fort) at Delhi.
His tomb is located at *Sasaram (Bihar)*.
After his death, his son Jalal Khan ruled (took the title Islam Shah Suri).
One of the *greatest rulers* of Mughal Dynasty.
*Eldest son* of Humayun.
*Full name: Abu'l-Fath Jalal-ud-din Muhammad Akbar*.
Akbar ascended the throne at the age of *13* at *Kalanaur, Punjab*.

*Bairam Khan*, who was the regent and tutor of *Akbar* led the Mughal forces in the *2nd Battle of Panipat* in *1556* which was fought between him and *Hemu* (the Hindu General of Adil Shah Suri). Hemu was defeated, captured and slain.
Akbar tried to win over the Rajputs wherever possible and inducted Rajput kings into Mughal service and treated them at par with Mughal nobility.
*Married: Harka Bai/Jodha Bai* (daughter of *Bharmal* → *Kachhwaha Rajput* ruler of *Amer*). She was later honoured with title “*Mariam-uz-Zamani*” after she gave birth to Jahangir.
*Rana Pratap Singh* and son *Amar Singh* (Sisodiya Rajputs of Mewar, Capital: Chittor) did not recognize his supremacy.
*This resulted in:*

Battle of Haldighati (1576): Between Maharana Pratap (Sisodiya Rajputs of Mewar, Capital: Chittor) and the Mughal Army led by Raja Man Singh of Amer. Rana Pratap and his son Amar Singh did not recognize Mughal supremacy. Rana Pratap never surrendered.
*Ibadat Khana:* Established a *“House of Worship”* for religious discussions.
*Sulh-i-Kul:* Introduced the policy of *“Universal Peace”* or religious tolerance.
*Din-i-Ilahi (1582):* A new syncretic religion/code of conduct proclaimed by Akbar.
It synthesized values from Islam, Hinduism, Jainism, Christianity, and Zoroastrianism.
**Note:** Birbal was the only Hindu to follow this religion.
**Failure:** It was not successful as a mass religion and failed after Akbar's death.
*Abul Fazal* described Akbar as "Insan-e-Kamil" (Perfect Man).
Jizya had been abolished by Akbar in 1564 AD.
*Agra Fort (1565 AD)*.
*Lahore Fort (1566 AD)*.

**Structures inside Fatehpur Sikri:**
*Buland Darwaza (1575 AD):* Built to commemorate his victory over the Gujarat expedition (1573 AD).
*Salim Chisti's Tomb:* Built by Akbar, but later redone in Marble by Jahangir.
**Other Palaces:** Palace of Birbal and Palace of Tansen (Akbar's Navratna) are also situated inside Fatehpur Sikri.
*Panch Mahal:* Also located in Fatehpur Sikri. Its Architectural pattern is based on Buddhist Vihara.
*Diwan-i-Khas:* Hall of Private Audiences, Built by Akbar.
*Allahabad fort (1585 AD)*

*2nd:* It is about reign of Akbar.

Akbar built a Translation Department (*Maktab Khana*).

→ Revenue was fixed based on the average yield of the land *over the past 10 years estimates* known as *Dahsala*, also known as Zabti system. It was introduced by Todar Mal.
**The land was divided into 4 types:**
*Polaj:* The most fertile and productive land, cultivated regularly without being left fallow.
*Parauti:* Land left uncultivated for *1-2 years* to regain its fertility.
*Chachar:* Land left fallow for *3-4 years* before being cultivated again.
*Banjar:* Land that remained uncultivated for *5 years or more*.

blocks.text: He started Mansabdari system in 1571. The word Mansab means rank or position which determined the status, salary and number of soldiers the Mansabdars were required to maintain.

items: Mansabs were graded on a numerical scale, with lowest rank being to *10* and highest reaching *5000 (later raised to 7000 and 10,000)*.
Each Mansabs were assigned *Zat*, representing their personal rank.
Mansabdars were often payed in Jagirs (land assignments) from where they had the right to collect revenue from those lands.
Military commanders in Akbar's empire: *Faujdars*.
Mansabdars holding *1000* or above Sawar rank were designated as Nobles *(Umara)*.
Raja Man Singh had a Mansab of 7000 (Sapth Hajari).
The Mansabdari system was not hereditary.
Mansabdars were paid in cash or Jagirs (land assignments with revenue rights).
Akbar defeated *Meeran Bahadur*, ruler of *Khandesh*.
In *1601*, Akbar carried out his last expedition in *Fort of Aseergarh of Khandesh* (part of Ahmednagar). Akbar won, however his son Jahangir revolted against him.
*He died in: 1605 → Tomb of Akbar in Sikandra, Agra*.
*Original name: Nur-ud-Din Muhammad Salim/Prince Salim* (son of Harka Bai).
**Birth:** 1569 AD    **Died:** 1627 AD
*Established: Zanjir-i-Adal* (i.e. Chain of Justice) at *Agra Fort* for seekers of royal justice. Jahangir was known for his strict administration of justice.
In *1611 AD*, he married *Mehr-un-Nissa* (widow of Sher Afghan, a Persian nobleman of Bengal)
Later known as *Nur Jahan*.
She was made official *Badshah Begum*.
She exercised tremendous influence over the state affairs.
Jahangir also issued silver coins in honour of his wife Mehr-un-Nissa.
He also married *Manmati Bai* (also known as Jagat Gosai) who was a Kachwaha princess. They gave birth to son *Prince Khurram* also known as “*Shah Jahan*”.
The tomb of *Itimad-ud-Daulah*, built by *Nur Jahan* in fond memory of her father, inlaid with *Pietra Dura* decoration is located in *Agra*.
The *Sarai of Nurmahal* is centrally protected monument of India, it is situated in Punjab constructed under the patronage of Nur Jahan.

In 1657 AD, his failing health set off the war of succession among his sons *Dara Shikoh, Aurangzeb, Murad Baksh*. In July 1658 AD, his son Aurangzeb emerged to be victorious.
Aurangzeb imprisoned his father in *Agra Fort* where he died in captivity in 1666 AD. He was buried at *Taj Mahal* (Agra) next to his wife Mumtaz’s tomb.
*Reason for Shah Jahan’s imprisonment*
Shah Jahan formally confirmed *Dara Shikoh as his heir*, granting him the title of *Shahzada-e-Buland Iqbal* (“Prince of High Fortune”) and wanted him to ascend the throne.
In 1658 AD, Aurangzeb defeated his elder brother Dara Shikoh (who was supported by Jaswant Singh of Marwar) in the following battles:
In the Battle of *Dharmat(1658), Samugarh(1658), & Deorai(1659)*, Aurangzeb emerged victorious and executed his brother Dara.
*After Victory:* He was crowned at Delhi and assumed the title *“Alamgir”* (Conqueror of the World).
Aurangzeb was known for his orthodox nature.
He captured *Guru Teg Bahadur* (9th Guru of Sikhs) and executed him because Guru Teg Bahadur refused to embrace Islam.
Aurangzeb moved to the Deccan in 1682 AD to control the region, which eventually led to the empire's decline.
**Annexation:**
- *Bijapur:* Annexed in 1686 AD.
- *Golconda:* Annexed in 1687 AD.
He appointed "*Muhtasibs*" (Censors of Public Morals) to enforce Islamic law and check un-Islamic practices.
*He wrote: Fatwa-i-Alamgiri* (Muslim Laws).
He re-introduced the Jizya tax on non-Muslims in 1679 AD.
*Death: 1707 AD* at *Devgiri, Aurangabad* (present day known as *Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar*).
*Buried at: Khuldabad* (Daulatabad).

*Built by:* Azam Shah (son of Aurangzeb) in fond memory of his mother Dilras Banu Begum who was posthumously named as Rabia-ud-Durrani.

items: He is also known as *Shah Alam I* or *Muazzam*.

He ascended the throne with the help of the Sayyid Brothers (Abdullah Khan & Hussain Ali Khan) who were also known as King Makers.

In 1717, he issued the Golden Farman to the British East India Company, granting duty-free trade in Bengal on payment of an annual sum of ₹ 3,000 and permitting the Company to issue Dastaks (trade passes).
*Sayyid Brothers* killed Farrukh Siyar with the help of *Marathas* (Peshwa Balaji Vishwanath).

He was also known as *“Rangeela”* due to his luxurious lifestyle.

During his reign, governors declared independence:
- **Awadh:** Founded by Saadat Khan (Burhan-ul-Mulk).
- *Battle of Karnal:* Muhammad Shah called Nizam-ul-Mulk and Saadat Khan for help, but they were defeated and captured.
- Nadir Shah (Ruler of Iran - Afsharid dynasty) sacked Delhi and took away the Peacock Throne, Kohinoor Diamond and immense amount of wealth.
This invasion was followed by a series of plundering raids by the Afghan ruler *Ahmad Shah Abdali*, who invaded north India *five times* between *1748 AD* and *1761 AD*.
He faced repeated invasions by the Afghan ruler Ahmed Shah Abdali.
He was also killed by nobles.
The famous *Battle of Plassey (1757)* was fought during his reign.
He was blinded by his nobles.
*Battle of Buxar (1764):* He fought against the British and lost.

He gave the title of "Raja" to the social reformer *Ram Mohan Roy*.
The last Mughal ruler.
Known as *Bahadur Shah Zafar*.

*Sarkar:* District (Headed by *Fauzdar*).

Under the subsidiary alliance, Indian rulers had to pay subsidy for the British Garrison and British Army deployed in their territory. They had to seek permission from British EIC to partner with any other European for any service, and also for the negotiations with other Indian rulers.

*Background:* In the 19th century, the Tsarist Empire (Russia) was expanding rapidly towards Central Asia. The British feared that Russia might attack India, which was the most important colony of the British Empire.
*Buffer State Policy:* It was considered necessary to make Afghanistan a buffer state to safeguard British India from external threats (especially Russia).
*Buffer State:* A state located between two powerful countries to prevent direct conflict between them.
*Ring Fence Policy:* *Warren Hastings* adopted this policy in 1765.
Objective: To create a protective ring of friendly/allied states around Company territories to safeguard them from external threats.
Nature: Defensive policy (focused on protection rather than expansion).
*Forward Policy:* Associated with *Lord Auckland*.
It was believed that if Russia advanced towards India, it was better to intervene in Afghanistan beforehand.
This policy led to British intervention in Afghanistan, resulting in the First Anglo-Afghan War.
The Tripartite Treaty was signed in 1838 under the Forward Policy of Lord Auckland.
This treaty was between three parties:
1. British Government
2. Shuja Shah Durrani (Afghan Ruler)
3. Ranjit Singh (Sikh Ruler)
To remove Dost Mohammad Khan and install Shuja Shah on the throne of Afghanistan.
To bring Afghanistan under British influence.
Fought during the tenure of *Lord Auckland* (Governor-General of India).
Initially, the British succeeded in removing *Dost Mohammad Khan* and placed *Shah Shuja Durrani* on the throne of Afghanistan.
However, this success was short-lived and a rebellion led to the collapse of British control.
Result: The British were defeated and forced to retreat from Kabul (1842); the retreat was nearly annihilated, marking one of the worst defeats in British Indian history.
Later, *John Lawrence* (Governor-General) adopted the *Policy of Masterly Inactivity* towards Afghanistan.
*Treaty of Gandamak* was signed between the British Government and Afghan ruler *Yakub Khan*.
Afghanistan's foreign policy went under British control.
Some frontier areas were given to British India
Lord Lytton implemented the "Proud Reserve Policy" of Britain.
The Durand Line was drawn in 1893 to define the boundary between Afghanistan and British India. This laid the foundation for today's Afghanistan-Pakistan border.
*Between:* British India and Afghan Ruler *Amir Abdur Rahman Khan*.

blocks.text: __Reformist Movements started in India due to:__

items: 1. Social evils in society that existed in forms of untouchability, superstition, polygamy (multiple marriages), purdah system, polytheism (worship of more than one God), etc.
2. Position of women deteriorated in society due to polygamy, Sati system and also because widow remarriage was not allowed.
*1829:* An Act passed to abolish *Sati System* with the efforts of *Raja Ram Mohan Roy* (*Lord William Bentinck* was the then *Governor-General of India*).
*1856:* *Widow Remarriage Act* passed with efforts of *Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar* [Got this title from his college (Principal in Sanskrit College)]
The Act was drafted during the tenure of Lord Dalhousie and enacted in 1856 under Lord Canning.
Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar was against child marriage.

*Sarda Act of 1929:* It is also known as *Child Marriage Restraint Act* through which minimum age for marriage at *14 years* for *girls* and *18 years* for *boys* was set.
*1835:* *Thomas Babington Macaulay* presented *Macaulay's Minute* to promote English education in India.
He gave *Downward Filtration Theory*.
Only rich Indians were to be given education in English medium.
*Lord William Bentinck* (Father of English Education in India) was the Governor-General of India during this time.
*1854:* *Wood's Dispatch*
It rejected the Downward Filtration Theory.
It is also known as *Magna Carta of Indian Education*.

blocks.caption: LAND REVENUE SYSTEM DURING BRITISH INDIA — map: Mahalwari System (Punjab & UP), Ryotwari System (Bombay & Madras), Permanent System (Bengal, Bihar & Orrisa)

items: *Introduced by:* **Lord Cornwallis**.
Planned by: **John Shore**.
It is also known as **"Zamindari System"**.
Under the Zamindari system, the land revenue was collected from the farmers by the intermediaries known as Zamindars (the middle man).
*It was introduced in:* **Bengal, Bihar, Orissa, Uttar Pradesh, and Madhya Pradesh**.

blocks.text: *A sunset clause was introduced in the Zamindari system that required zamindars to pay afixed amount of revenue by a specific date, before sunset. If the zamindars failed to pay the revenue on time, the government would confiscate and auction off their zamindari, which was hereditary.*

items: Introduced by: **Thomas Munro** and **Captain Alexander Read**.
It was based on **Ricardian Theory of Rent**.
Under the Ryotwari system *(Ryot: Peasant)*, the land revenue was paid by the farmers directly to the state.
*It was introduced in:* **South India** (Madras region of Tamil Nadu).
Introduced by: **Holt Mackenzie** (Concept) and later reformed by **William Bentinck** (1833).
*It was introduced in*: North-Western Provinces, Central India, Punjab, and parts of Uttar Pradesh (Gangetic Valley).
Under the Mahalwari system, the land revenue was collected from the farmers *by the village headmen on behalf of the whole village* (and not the zamindar).
The village or group of villages was termed a **"Mahal"** and treated as a single revenue unit.

items: 1. *Champaran Satyagrah* (1917) → __First Civil Disobedience Movement__

*Raj Kumar Shukla* invited Gandhi to *Champaran, Bihar* to complain about injustice of the landlord system where the *farmers were forced to cultivate Indigo in 3/20th part of their land, also known as Tinkathia System.*
*Other people involved in Champaran Satyagraha: Rajendra Prasad, Mazhar-ul-Haq, J. B. Kriplani.*
This act was a direct result of Champaran Satyagraha. It abolished Tinkathia system and suspended *Abwab tax (an illegal collection imposed by landlords on peasants).*
2. *Ahmedabad Mill Strike* (1918) → __First Hunger Strike__
*Anusuya Sarabhai* invited Gandhi to *Ahmedabad* where textile workers of mill in Ahmedabad fought for economic injustice when the mill owners discontinued their *Plague Bonus*.
Workers *demanded a 50% wage hike* to compensate for rising costs of living. *The mill owners offered only a 20% raise* which led to the strike. Finally wage hike was settled at 35%.
3. *Kheda Satyagrah* (1918) → __First Non-Cooperation__
This Satyagraha movement was carried out at *Kheda district of Gujarat* in support of the peasants who were not able to pay the land tax due to the failure of crops.
**Result:** Taxes for the current and next year were waived, confiscated properties were returned to farmers, and the increased revenue rate was reduced.
It is also known as *Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms*.
*Montagu was the Secretary and Chelmsford was the Viceroy.*
1. It extended *separate electorate* to Anglo Indians, Christians.
2. It introduced *dyarchy at provinces* → Provincial subjects were divided into two parts.

The Rowlatt Satyagraha was a non-violent protest movement *led by Mahatma Gandhi against the Rowlatt Act (1919)*, which was passed by the British government to curb revolutionary activities in India. He called this Act the *"Black Act"*, the act sought to suspend the law of *Habeas Corpus*, which protects a person's right against unlawful detention.
It was suggested by *Sydney Rowlatt (a British Judge)*.
It was officially called the *Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act, 1919*.
It allowed the British government to *detain people without trial and imprison them for 2 years without trial*.
It was an extension of the *Defence of India Act, 1915*, a World War I time law, even though the World War I had already ended.
It took place on *13 April 1919 (on Baisakhi)*.
In *Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar* people gathered in mass for two reasons: *To celebrate Baisakhi* and *to protest against the arrest of Saifuddin Kitchlew* and *Dr. Satyapal*.
Without warning, Brigadier General Reginald Dyer, accompanied by his armed troops, closed the only exit gate and open fired at the crowd.
Official records stated 379 deaths, but actual numbers were over 1000.

blocks.text: __Suspension of the Movement and Political Vaccum__

items: In Bardoli, Congress working committee suspended the non-cooperation movement due to violence and focused on constructive work from now onwards.
This led to a political vacuum within the Congress party, as leaders were divided on what to do next.

*No Changers:* This group, led by *Rajendra Prasad, Vallabhbhai Patel, and M.A. Ansari*, believed in continuing Gandhi's principles. They *boycotted the British councils and instead focused on constructive work like setting up ashrams, schools, and promoting Khadi* (hand-spun cloth).

*Pro Changers:* It was led by *C.R. Das, Motilal Nehru, and Ajmal Khan*, this group argued that entering the legislative councils would allow them to challenge British rule from within by opposing unfair laws and policies. However, Gandhi initially opposed this idea, as he felt cooperating with the British in any form would weaken the freedom struggle.
In the *Gaya Session of Congress (Dec 1922)*, the Pro Changers decided to form their own political party the *'Congress Khilafat Swaraj Party'* or *'Swaraj Party'*.
*Leadership: C.R. Das* → President; *Motilal Nehru* → Secretary.
*Goal: "To end or mend the Council".*
Achievement: Successfully defeated the *"Public Safety Bill"* in *1928*.

items: In *1940*, *August Offer* was sent by Lord Linlithgow under the direction of *British Prime Minister Winston Churchill* to secure India's cooperation in World War II. The key Provisions were:
1. The British Government promised to grant Dominion Status for India.
2. Indians could create their own Constituent Assembly themselves with inclusion of more Indian representatives than whites.
3. An advisory war council was to be established.
4. Veto for Minorities: No future constitution would be adopted without the consent of the minorities.
→ However these provisions were rejected by Congress as Congress had declared *"Purna Swaraj"* as their goal.
→ Jawaharlal Nehru completely rejected the British August Offer of 1940, calling it *"dead as a doornail"*.
→ The Muslim League also rejected this proposal.

The INC decided to launch the *"Individual Satyagraha"* after the failure of August Offer under the leadership of *Mahatma Gandhi* in *1940* with *Vinoba Bhave* nominated as *first Satyagrahi*, *J L Nehru* as *second* and *Brahma Dutt* as *the third*.
*Objective:* To protest against India's forced participation in World War II and to assert the right to freedom of speech, without causing mass disruption to British war efforts.
*Threat of Japan:* Rapid Japanese expansion in Southeast Asia and advance up to Burma created fear of invasion of India.
*International Pressure:* After the Pearl Harbor attack, Franklin D. Roosevelt and Allied nations pressured Britain to grant political concessions to India.
*Attack on Russia:* Operation Barbarossa by Germany against the Soviet Union (1941) worsened Britain's war position, increasing its urgent need for Indian support (men and resources).
In *1942*, *Stafford Cripps* was sent to India with a constitutional reforms. The key proposals were:
1. A Constituent Assembly would be formed to frame a new constitution for the country. This Assembly would have members elected by the provincial assemblies and also nominated by Princely States.
2. India would be granted Dominion Status, allowing self-governance while remaining part of the British Commonwealth.
3. Any province unwilling to join the Indian dominion could form a separate union and have a separate constitution.
4. The transfer of power and the rights of minorities would be safeguarded.
The proposals were rejected by INC. Gandhi famously referred Cripps offer of Dominion Status as *"post-dated cheque drawn on a failing bank"*.
*July 1942:* *The Congress Working Committee* met at *Wardha, Maharashtra* where *Quit India Resolution* was adopted. It was ratified at *Gowalika Tank, Bombay*.

On *8th August 1942*, *Mahatma Gandhi launched Quit India Movement* (also known as *August Kranti*) in response to the failure of Cripps Mission. Which was initially not supported by J L Nehru and Hindu Mahasabha.

The *"Do or Die"* slogan was given *during a speech by Mahatma Gandhi* at the *Gowalia Tank Maidan* or *August Kranti Maidan* in *Mumbai*. The slogan was a call to action for Indians to fight for independence from British rule.
The very first day all leaders got arrested and that is why this movement was a *leaderless movement*.
*Aruna Asaf Ali* presided over Congress Working Committee, she also hoisted the Indian Flag at the Gowalika Tank in Mumbai.

items: *Objective:* Transfer of power and constitution-making.
The plan proposed a united India, rejecting the demand for a separate Pakistan.
The Cabinet Mission Plan proposed a three-tier administrative structure for British India:
- *Federal Union* at the top level,
- *Groups of provinces* at the middle level,
- *Individual provinces* at the bottom level.
*Provincial were divided into three groups:*
*Group A:* Hindu-majority provinces (Madras, Bombay, UP, Bihar, Odisha, Central Provinces).
*Group B:* Muslim-majority provinces (Punjab, NWFP, Sindh, Balochistan).
*Group C:* Bengal and Assam.
Central Government to have power over defense, foreign affairs, and communications.
Provinces to have full autonomy and residual powers.
Princely States were free to negotiate their status, either to join with Britishers or Successor Governments.
Constituent Assembly to draft a new constitution; members would be elected by provincial assemblies.
Interim Government to function till the new constitution and government were formed.

However, when Muhammad Ali Jinnah realized that grouping was not strictly mandatory, he called for *"Direct Action Day" (16 August 1946)* demanding Pakistan, this led to horrific communal violence in Calcutta, known as the *"Great Calcutta Killing."*

Sher Ali Afridi killed him in Andaman Jail.
He brought **Vernacular Press Act (1878)** to ban local newspapers.
**First Delhi Durbar (1877)** to proclaim Queen Victoria as Empress of India.
He lowered the maximum age of eligibility for Civil Services (reduced the age from 21 to 19 years).

blocks.text: *"Bengal Gazette" by James Augustus Hickey was the 1st Newspaper in India (1780).*

items: **Ilbert bill** was introduced in 1883.
He repealed **Vernacular Press Act**.
**1st Synchronised (complete) Census** held during his tenure (1881).
He is known as **Father of Local Self-Government**.
**Hunter Commission (1882)** was setup which was related to Education.
**1st Factory Act (1881)**.
**Formation of INC (1885)**.
**Partition of Bengal (1905)**.
**Indian Universities Act**.
**Calcutta Corporation Act**.
**Curzon-Kitchener controversy**.
**Younghusband Mission** (to Tibet).
**Muslim League formation (1906)** by Aga Khan.
**Surat Split**.
**3rd Delhi Durbar** for King George (1911).
During his tenure, **capital shifted from Calcutta to Delhi**.
**Royal Commission on Civil Services** was appointed in 1912.
**Government of India Act**, also known as **Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (1919)**.
**Rowlatt Act (1919)**.
**Jallianwala Bagh Massacre (1919)**.

**Kakori Train Incident (1925)**.

Repeal of **Press Act (1910)** and **Rowlatt Act (1919)**.

**3rd Round Table Conference**.
